metadata.title: Some States Stepping In: Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes During the Financial Crisis

abstract: At the core of the 2008 global financial crisis was a foreclosure crisis in the United States. The federal government focused on responding to the concurrent banking crisis, leaving foreclosure prevention to the states. Despite a nationwide crisis, only some states advanced foreclosure prevention policies. Political theory scholars argue that political ideology and economic interests are the primary drivers of policy outcomes, while discourse scholars argue that themes in the public discourse shape policymaking. In this article, I integrate these literatures to develop and test an account of foreclosure prevention policymaking. To measure discourse, I scrape the text of over 20,000 statelevel media publications and inductively code them using Structural Topic Modeling. Using event history analysis, I examine the relationship between discourse themes, political factors, and the timing of foreclosure prevention policymaking by state legislatures. I find that states where a "markets" theme was more prevalent in the foreclosure discourse were less likely to advance foreclosure prevention policies, whereas states with discourse focused on "intervention" were more likely to do so. Results also corroborate previous scholarship showing that political ideology and special interest group activity impacted these policy outcomes.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: At the core of the 2008 global financial crisis was a collapse in the housing markets in the United States, with an ensuing foreclosure crisis (Eichengreen 2015;Shiller 2012). The federal government's response to the foreclosure crisis has been characterized by scholars as "too little, too late, and too timid" (Immergluck 2013; see also Blinder 2013), leaving state governments to manage the foreclosure crisis largely on their own (Walsh 2011). During the crisis, the states enacted over 300 foreclosure-related laws, vastly changing the foreclosure legal landscape (Fox 2014). Yet, only some states advanced legislation that aimed to prevent current foreclosures; this type of legislation included mortgage payment assistance plans and programs that mandated and funded counseling and mediation. Other states, though, did little or nothing to stem current foreclosures. Why did only some states advance foreclosure prevention policies during a nationwide crisis? Scholars of policy discourse argue that ideas and themes that dominate the public discourse about policy problems and potential policy solutions can affect the success or failure of policy outcomes (Béland 2005;Campbell 2002;Ferree 2002Ferree , 2003;;Padamsee 2009;Steensland 2006). In line with this, observers of the foreclosure crisis may have noticed passionate and conflicting rhetoric regarding the foreclosure crisis and what to do about it. One theme is illustrated by CNBC host Risk Santelli's comment, "This is America! How many of you people want to pay for your neighbors' mortgage that has an extra bathroom and can't pay their bills?" 1 Another theme is illustrated by Elizabeth Warren's expression of sympathy for borrowers facing foreclosure by comparing unregulated mortgages to unregulated toasters that could burn down someone's house. 2 While such rhetoric made headlines, did it impact policy outcomes?

The policy discourse literature consists of rich theoretical development and empirical qualitative research that examines how discourse can affect policy outcomes, but it rarely tests discourse theories against other explanatory factors (Steensland 2006). This leaves policy discourse theory open to the assumption that it is, at best, of secondary importance behind economic interests and political factors (Bernstein 1989;Campbell 2002;Peltzman 1985;Poole and Rosenthal 1996;Stigler 1971). Sometimes scholars working from the political factors perspective acknowledge discourse and vice versa. Soule and Zylan (1997), for example, share quotes of the fiery rhetoric about the policy that they analyze (Aid to Families with Dependent Children [AFDC]), but the discourse is absent from their theory and empirical analysis. While Ferree (2002), in her study of abortion policy discourse, notes that policymakers may be influenced by political factors in addition to cultural frames, the study focuses on the discourse. The key theoretical and empirical aim of this paper is to integrate these literatures. This step enables establishing whether discourse and political factors can have independent effects on policymaking and helps in beginning to disentangle when these different forces may be influential in shaping policy outcomes.

The political factors scholarship shows that several key factors can affect policy outcomes. Political ideology affects outcomes in that, all else equal, conservative policymakers are less likely to advance social support policies, while liberal policymakers are more likely to advance them (Bernstein 1989;Gross, Medvetz, and Russell 2011;Lo and Schwartz 1998;Poole and Rosenthal 1996). This suggests that during the foreclosure crisis, states dominated by liberal legislators would be the ones to advance foreclosure prevention policies. However, policymakers may support policies according to the economic interests of their constituents, in order to be reelected, regardless of the policymakers' own ideological position (Peltzman 1985;Stigler 1971). In this case, states suffering a steeper housing market decline should be the ones advancing foreclosure prevention policies. Even considering these two political factors together -ideology and economic interests -cannot fully explain the pattern of states' foreclosure prevention policy actions during the crisis. For example, why did states like North Carolina and Pennsylvania, with relatively conservative legislatures and whose housing markets experienced relatively little decline, nevertheless advance aggressive foreclosure prevention legislation, while a state like Rhode Island, which turned more liberal after the 2010 midterm elections and had a relatively severe housing downturn, did not enact any foreclosure prevention legislation? sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: In this paper, I test whether emerging foreclosure crisis discourse can help explain states' legislative foreclosure prevention policy outcomes, while also accounting for political factors. I use event history analysis to examine the relationship between discourse themes, political factors, and when states introduce and pass foreclosure prevention policies. To measure themes in the discourse, I scrape the text of over 20,000 state-level media publications and inductively code them using Structural Topic Modeling (Roberts et al. 2014). I find that two conflicting themes emerge -a "market" and an "intervention" theme. These themes reflect a preexisting discursive structure regarding markets and state intervention and I discuss them in greater detail below (Campbell 2002:32; see also Block 1996;Campbell and Pedersen 2001;Fourcade and Healy 2007;Lee Mudge 2008;Prasad 2006). The model also integrates political factors including state-level housing price declines, the dominant political ideology of legislatures and the public, as well as the presence of special interest groups.

The findings indicate that the two discourse themes have opposing effects: states with the intervention theme prevalent in their discourse are more likely to advance foreclosure prevention policy, while states with the market theme prevalent are less likely to do so. These findings provide evidence that the discourse surrounding a policy problem can affect policy outcomes, independently of what we might expect considering straightforward economic and political factors alone.

sections.header_raw: Introducing the Case and Existing 2008 Financial Crisis Policy Research
sections.header_norm: introducing the case and existing 2008 financial crisis policy research
paragraphs: Housing prices began to decline toward the end of 2006, led by states that had the strongest housing price growth during the preceding years -the "sun states," like Arizona and California. By the spring of 2007, policymakers across the country were on alert, nearly every state's housing prices declined, and mortgage defaults and foreclosures increased. Before the crisis was over, the national median home price dropped by 24% and about 8 million households lost their homes to foreclosure. This contributed to a destruction of household wealth, especially among the middle class (Wolff 2016), and not only affected the households involved but also had spillover effects across neighborhoods (Wassmer 2011).

Over the five years from the beginning of 2007 until the end of 2011, when the foreclosure crisis began to subside, there was considerable variation in how state legislatures responded. Some advanced policies that aimed to prevent current foreclosures; some focused on policies that would regulate future lending to prevent future crises, but did not attempt to prevent current foreclosures; and others advanced policies that more simply tried to clean up their foreclosure process. In some states, like Kansas, legislatures introduced only a few bills related to foreclosure in any way and passed none, while in other states, like Illinois, legislatures introduced and passed many bills.

The focus in the current paper is on legislation that aggressively aimed at preventing current foreclosures. 3 Maine's legislature, for example, quickly introduced and passed a financial assistance program by June 2007, giving its housing authority the power to pay off a borrower's mortgage. Other states took longer, but nevertheless advanced foreclosure prevention policies. North Carolina provided funding for approved foreclosure counseling organizations in August 2008 and New Jersey, by January 2009, offered financial incentives for lenders to refinance mortgages.

Although there is already considerable previous scholarship on the financial crisis policies, much of this work is on the federal policy interventions in the banking crisis (Cecchetti 2008(Cecchetti , 2009;;Croci, Hertig, and Nowak 2016;Swagel 2009Swagel , 2015)), while less work has examined the federal policies aimed at the foreclosure crisis. In an exception, Mian, Sufi, and Trebbi (2010) try to disentangle the effects of different political factors by examining how the number of mortgage defaults and legislator political ideology affected congressional voting on the federal 2008 Foreclosure Prevention Act. The current paper builds on this by examining state-level policymaking and integrating public discourse. 4 Existing crisis policy research on state responses to the foreclosure crisis is limited (Alexander et al. 2011;Crump et al. 2008). Yet, understanding why some states advanced foreclosure prevention policies is important since this type of crisis social support policy response is often primarily the policy purview of the states, as was the case during the 2008 financial crisis (Immergluck 2013), the Great Depression (Amenta and Poulsen 1996), and the COVID-19 pandemic (Béland et al. 2021). Further, state-level policymaking in general has become increasingly divergent across states and is having increasing effects on many aspects of people's lives (Basseches, Campbell, and Schoenfeld 2024;Merriman and Pacewicz 2022).

paragraphs: Previous research on policy outcomes is largely separated into two streams. This section will first discuss the political factors approach, which includes economic interests, both constituent and special interests, and political ideology; then the second stream, which emphasizes public discourse and how the themes and ideas in it can shape people's understandings of policy problems and solutions. Integrating and empirically testing these theories together is the primary goal of the current paper.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: Previous literature effectively documents a role for political factors in shaping policy outcomes; this includes political ideology (Bernstein 1989;Poole and Rosenthal 1996) and economic interests (Peltzman 1985;Stigler 1971). A baseline in this perspective is that policy preferences are shaped by material economic interests (Campbell 2002). All else equal, constituents prefer policies that improve their economic situation (Peltzman 1984;Savage 2019). Elected policymakers' want to be reelected, which can align their interests with their voters' (Downs 1957;Maestas 2003;Mian, Sufi, and Trebbi 2010;Peltzman 1985), though wealthy constituents often have more political influence (Beckert 2022;Gilens 2012). In a foreclosure crisis, we might expect that state legislatures in states facing a more severe housing crisis would be more supportive of foreclosure prevention policies to help their constituents manage the economic crisis.

A variation on this argument, however, is that it may be special interests rather than constituents' interests that influence policymakers' policy actions (Mian, Sufi, and Trebbi 2010). Special interest groups can have a big impact on policymaking, and this may be especially true in state legislatures, which have limited legislative staffing and rely on interest groups for information and support (Grasse and Heidbreder 2011;Squire and Moncrief 2020: Chp. 3). One of the most effective ways that interest groups influence policymaking is by "lobbying," i.e., providing legislators with information on policy problems (Squire and Moncrief 2020:162; see also Fleisher 1993;Grasse and Heidbreder 2011;Langbein and Lotwis 1990). Of course, the information provided to policymakers from interest group representatives is not neutral but in line with the group's interests.

Two interest groups are particularly active in the mortgage and foreclosure policy arena. One is banking-related interest groups. These groups have "traditionally had a great deal of influence over state legislatures" (Immergluck 2009:473). During the foreclosure crisis, banks were concerned about any foreclosure prevention laws that might result in additional mortgage loan losses, because banks were already facing large write-downs of their balance sheets as more and more mortgage borrowers defaulted (Furlong and Knight 2010). sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: The "cultural turn" in policy scholarship addresses an assumption in political theory by arguing that policy outcomes do not always follow straightforwardly from economic and political interests, but instead can be shaped by the ideas and cultural meanings constituting the discourse (Blyth 2002;Béland 2005;Campbell 2002;Padamsee 2009;Steensland 2006;Steinmetz 1999). 8 As an example, if the discourse on potential policies stigmatizes policy beneficiaries, then even those who would benefit may not voice support for the policy (Boushey 2016;Fabrizio, Shipan, and Wüest 2021;Steensland 2006).

Themes in the discourse can shape eventual policy outcomes by shaping what happens at different stages in the policymaking process. A first stage is the problem definition or agenda-setting stage (Béland 2016). Much of the political factors literature tends to start with situations that have already been defined as policy problems. However, a fundamental part of policy debates is often whether a situation is one that "must [and] can" be dealt with through policy (Hay 2001:203, emphasis in original; see also Stone 1989). The emerging discourse needs to identify the situation as a policy problem, rather than a situation that is unproblematic, or one that may be problematic but nevertheless is one in which the state cannot or should not intervene. Klinenberg (2015) shows how heat waves causing hundreds of deaths occur without being identified as a policy problem. Crisis policymaking scholarship also tends to take for granted that a crisis will be perceived as a policy problem (e.g. Boin, 't Hart, and Allan 2009; see also Schranz and Eisenegger 2011). However, the scholarship on the social construction of social and policy problems emphasizes that such perceptions should not be taken for granted (Auyero and Swistun 2008;Hay 2001;Stone 1989;Tierney 2007;Wagner-Pacifici 2017) and emerging discourse can impact this problem definition stage, affecting the chances that the policymaking process advances.

A second stage occurs if a situation is defined as a policy problem. In this stage, different policy solutions are developed and debated (Béland 2016). Policymakers, experts, and interest group representatives try to frame the problem and their solution in ways they believe will increase their chances of success (Campbell 2002). Participants in this stage try to shape the discourse using ideas and cultural meanings (Ferree 2002(Ferree , 2003;;Steensland 2006). While we can analytically imagine these separate stages of policymaking, in reality they may be intertwined in the discourse. Even in situations that gain widespread attention, different definitions of the problem may imply "wildly divergent" solutions, including nonaction, and framing debates over policy solutions that include non-or limited action can occur simultaneously in the discourse with the struggles over problem definition (Hay 2001:204; see also Anderson 2013;Gusfield 1981).

For economic problems in particular, there is a dominant existing discourse that makes the case that the best solution is to allow markets to resolve the problem and for the state not to intervene; this discourse is often referred to by scholars as neo-liberalism (Lee Mudge 2008) or market fundamentalism (Block and Somers 2014). This discourse centers "free" markets as a better solution to economic problems, in contrast to state intervention. Within this discourse, interventions by the state in the market will usually result in negative consequences (Block 1996), including perversely exacerbating the very economic problem the state is trying to address (Somers and Block 2005), or a loss of individual freedom (Fourcade and Healy 2007;Lee Mudge 2008).

As new events arise, participants can, but do not necessarily, draw on "pre-existing discursive structures," like neo-liberalism, to make sense of the new problem (Campbell 2002:32; see also Ferree 2003;Gamson and Modigliani 1989). 9 During this process of collective sense-making, some discursive themes will be more successful than others in shaping how the problem is understood and what responses are accepted as appropriate (Ellingson 1995: 101; see also Gamson and Modigliani 1989).

The media plays a critical role in this process of collective sense-making, as the "master forum" for discourse (Ferree 2002:10; see also Bail 2012;Gamson 2004). The media is the master forum because it can reach virtually everyone and anyone who wants to influence public opinion on a policy problem tries to use the media, making it the primary location of public discourse (Bail 2012;Ferree 2002;Gamson 2004). Further, policymakers know that interested social actors try to influence public opinion by influencing media coverage, so they cannot ignore the discourse there (Ferree 2002:10). In addition to assuming that media themes will shape public opinion, policymakers often have no better information source than the media on what public opinion is, and this is especially true regarding preferences on state-level legislation (Rosenthal 2004;Squire and Moncrief 2020). Media coverage both reflects and shapes the public's developing understanding of a policy problem and potential solutions (Ferree 2002;Gamson 2004;Sarathchandra and Haltinner 2023).

Media coverage also provides legislators with information on the unfolding situation, shaping their own understandings of the problem (Burstein 2003;Garlick 2017;Whiteman 1995). Legislators may be reading media publications themselves and, additionally, legislative staff often rely on media sources when writing reports for policymakers. For example, a staff report on foreclosures for the Kentucky legislature during the crisis cites numerous press releases and media articles. 10 Through these two routes -via the public and policymakers themselves -the discourse themes in the media can affect policy outcomes directly by shaping policymakers' own understandings of the problem and their policy preferences (Campbell 2002;Skrentny 2006;Steensland 2006) or indirectly by shaping and reflecting constituents' preferences (Ferree 2002:15). The current analysis cannot adjudicate between these two complementary mechanisms but instead assumes that either or both were at work during the foreclosure crisis. In this analysis, the public discourse plays the role of Koopmans and Olzak's (2004) discursive opportunity structures. Although the comparison is unfortunate, crisis legislators are not unlike Koopmans and Olzak's (2004) radical right activists, who hope to use the media as a mouthpiece, but also use it to gauge developing public opinion and make their own political action decisions accordingly. In the uncertain policymaking situation created by a crisis, when previous understandings and positions may be susceptible to change (Boin, 't Hart, and Allan 2009;Hay 2001), the emerging public discourse provides crucial information to policymakers about the unfolding crisis and the developing opinions about it and potential policy solutions. The empirical questions for the analysis are: 1) what foreclosure problem discourse themes developed in states' media and 2) are they associated with policy outcomes.

sections.preview_tail: Although the comparison is unfortunate, crisis legislators are not unlike Koopmans and Olzak's (2004) radical right activists, who hope to use the media as a mouthpiece, but also use it to gauge developing public opinion and make their own political action decisions accordingly. In the uncertain policymaking situation created by a crisis, when previous understandings and positions may be susceptible to change (Boin, 't Hart, and Allan 2009;Hay 2001), the emerging public discourse provides crucial information to policymakers about the unfolding crisis and the developing opinions about it and potential policy solutions. The empirical questions for the analysis are: 1) what foreclosure problem discourse themes developed in states' media and 2) are they associated with policy outcomes.

paragraphs: From the beginning of 2007 through the end of 2011, thirty-two state legislatures introduced 160 pieces of legislation to prevent current foreclosures, and eighteen states ultimately passed thirty-six such laws. 11 The foreclosure prevention legislation did at least one of two things. First, they could fund counseling and mediation programs for current borrowers. North Carolina, for example, provided funding for foreclosure counseling in August 2008. Second, they could provide financial assistance to current borrowers to help avoid foreclosure. Maine, in June 2007, gave its housing authority the power to pay off a borrower's mortgage. Other programs offer financial incentives for lenders to refinance mortgages, such as New Jersey's January 2009 program. Figure 1 shows each state and when they introduced (dotted vertical lines) or passed (solid vertical lines) foreclosure prevention legislation between 2007 and 2011. The figure also shows the housing market collapse in each state (roughly horizontal lines) and the political ideology of the state legislatures (red, yellow, blue background colors). Note that we think of some states as "red" or "blue" states based on their Congressional representatives; however, their state legislatures may be more moderate or lean in the opposite political direction.

As the starting point for the legislation data, I used the National Conference of State Legislatures' (NCSL) list of crisis foreclosure legislation, which includes synopses of the legislation. I triangulated this list using additional sources to ensure that the list was comprehensive. This process resulted in three additional pieces of legislation for Oregon.

Determining which legislation met my definition of aggressive foreclosure prevention legislation required additional work, including obtaining and reading the full text of the legislation and reading secondary sources. This was necessary for two reasons. One is because the NCLS's synopses were not accurate enough; for example, the NCLS included Alaska's S.B. 231 in 2008, which, according to the NCSL's synopsis, provided additional "financial assistance to prevent homelessness, including prevention of foreclosures and evictions." Based on this, the legislation seems to be funding foreclosure prevention. However, the full text of the legislation and a report about Alaska's homelessness prevention program reveals that the legislature did not advance this legislation to address the foreclosure crisis, but rather to continue an earlier homelessness program. 12 While some legislation adjusted on-going programs to address the foreclosure crisis and should be included, that was not the case for Alaska.

Another reason for the additional work beyond the NCLS's list is that not all the introduced legislation started out with foreclosure prevention included. Sometimes the relevant foreclosure prevention piece was added later, as an amendment. When this was the case, I read through each subsequent version of the bill until locating when foreclosure prevention was added to establish the correct dates for the event history analysis.

paragraphs: To measure themes in states' foreclosure crisis discourse, I collected newspaper articles and news website text using Lexis-Nexis academic and ProQuest (for similar methodological approaches, see Boushey 2016). The media data include articles from more traditional and well-known newspapers such as the Los Angeles Times and the Chicago Tribune, as well as less well-known outlets including Louisiana's New Orleans City Business and Maine's Kennebec Journal. These data also include web-based outlets such as Washington state's The B-Town Blog. I collect all articles on foreclosure in all states, whether they advanced any foreclosure prevention or not. This is necessary to measure the discourse in states that do not advance legislation as well as those that do. I do not collect data from social media platforms, but I note that the articles constituting my data are often shared and circulated on social media. To identify articles on the foreclosure crisis, I searched for articles that had the words "foreclosure" or "foreclosures" in the headline, and those that were published between 1/1/2007 and 12/31/2011. This resulted in 20,962 articles. See Appendix A Table A1 for the full list of media outlets and the number of articles from each source.

To inductively code and quantify the themes in these text data, I use an unsupervised form of topic modeling called Structural Topic Modeling (Roberts et al. 2014), which builds on the traditional Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) by incorporating meta data to improve the identification of topics. 13 The meta data I include are the year and state in which the article was published. Unsupervised topic modeling allows researchers to take advantage of an inductive coding approach that is often used in qualitative analyses (DiMaggio 2015; Evans and Aceves 2016;Nelson 2020;Nelson et al. 2018).

In implementing an unsupervised topic model, the researcher decides on the number of topics that the model will estimate but does not influence the content of the topics. Quantitative methods exist for choosing the number of topics (e.g. Lee and Mimno 2014), though social scientists note that the solutions with substantively meaningful topics are frequently not the mathematically optimal solutions (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013; Nelson etal. 2018). Instead, the common approach is to estimate several solutions with varying numbers of topics and identify the solution with analytically useful topics. I followed this approach, and after examining the results with various numbers of topics (5-25), I chose the 15-topic solution. Further details on this choice are in Appendix A Section A2.

Identifying and labeling what the inductively discovered topics reflect requires the researcher's interpretation. I establish and validate my topic labels by considering three pieces of information: 1) the words constituting each topic with the highest probability; 2) the top FREX terms, which are a variation on the most probable words that consider the relative salience of terms in a given topic (Hausser and Strimmer 2009); and 3) my reading of articles that have a high probability of being constituted by particular topics, which allowed me to assess whether the articles' substantive content reflects my topic interpretation. See Table 1 below for lists of the eleven most probable words for each of the fifteen topics. Table 1 groups the topics into three categories: policy problem topics, episodic events topics, and what I call idiosyncratic foreclosure language topics. I discuss these categorizations more in the following paragraphs. To more fully demonstrate the meanings of these topics, Appendix A Table A2 shows a list of the top FREX terms, excerpts from articles that have a high probability of being constituted by each topic, as well as one complete article and the probabilities that it is constituted by each of the topics. 14 In an unsupervised model, there is no guarantee that topics of any number will map onto a researchers' analytical goals, because the topics will correspond to what structures the corpus (Chuang et al. 2013;Eads et al. 2021;McAuliffe and Blei 2008). The benefit of an unsupervised approach is allowing the inductive discovery of themes (Nelson 2020;Nelson et al. 2021). In a corpus of news articles, the primary structure is episodic reporting on events and many of the topics will likely reflect that (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013). I interpreted over half of the topics in the 15-topic solution as episodic event topics. I labeled these topics "families," "documentation," "fraud," "abandoned property," "counseling," "foreclosure activity," "legislation," and "minority families." I interpreted five other topics as reflecting the idiosyncratic language involved in discussing foreclosures. I labeled these "CRE" for commercial real estate, "court," "counties," "home sales," and "filler." This last reflects less substantively meaningful words, akin to "stop" words. See Table 1 for the most probable words in these topics and Appendix A Section A2 for an extended discussion of how I labeled and categorized each.

Importantly for this paper, I interpreted two topics as reflecting either side of a market versus intervention discourse and identify these as the analytically relevant topics in this study. The "markets" topic reflects discussions of the policy problem, i.e., housing markets and foreclosures, in depoliticized and objective-sounding language (Fourcade and Healy 2007;Shiller 2017). Additionally, this topic reflects discussions of policy solutions that are critical of state intervention and supportive of natural market-based solutions. This type of discourse could affect the public and policymakers' understanding of the severity of the problem or their sense of connection with it (Shiller 2017). More directly, it could negatively

shape opinions about the appropriateness and effectiveness of legislative policy solutions.

The "intervention" topic is a counter topic to the markets topic in that it reflects discussions of the policy problem in relatively political and personal or nonobjective terms (Shiller 2017) and discussions of the policy solutions that involve intervention more favorably. This type of discourse could influence the public and policymakers' sympathy with borrowers and positively shape their perceptions of policy solutions. The expectation is that states with the markets theme more prevalent in their crisis discourse will be less likely to advance foreclosure prevention legislation, while those with the intervention theme more prevalent will be more likely to advance foreclosure prevention legislation. I illustrate the differences in the markets and intervention topics using excerpts from the media data. First, I show the depoliticized and objective-sounding components of the markets theme compared with the personal and blame-casting intervention theme. Then, I compare how articles on the markets theme discuss policy interventions negatively compared to positive intervention theme discussions. The depoliticized and objectives-sounding language in the markets theme include using basic economics concepts such as "supply" and "surplus" and using statistics instead of personal stories:

Overbuilding of new homes created a surplus that will take some time to work through. 15 Sobering new statistics released yesterday . . . Home foreclosures in Greater Lowell in 2007 have increased 155 percent through October. 16 Another form of depoliticized language that the markets topic reflects is discussions of the market as a natural force, often using nature or disease metaphors (Pühringer and Hirte 2015):

The economy is treading water, and the housing market is one of the undercurrents trying to pull it down. 17 "We're just getting toward the eye of the storm," said Guy Cecala, publisher of Inside Mortgage Finance. 18 Foreclosures dip in some of the hardest-hit counties, but in wealthier areas, the plague tightens its grip. 19 In contrast, the intervention theme captures discussions in which the problem is not seen as the, perhaps unfortunate, but natural workings of the market, but instead casts blame on specific social actors and tells personal stories that portray the borrowers as sympathetic:

Travis Munnerlyn just wanted to add on to his Orlando home to make room for grandkids coming to live with him and his wife. A couple of years later, he can't make the increasing payments on his adjustable-rate loan -not even close -and he could lose the house. 20 They say the mortgage industry's bad lending practices are mostly to blame. 21 One reason, both in Pueblo and nationally, is that many foreclosed home-buyers used adjustable-rate mortgages. The mortgages were often marketed to buyers who couldn't qualify for a better fixed-rate mortgage. 22 When articles on the markets theme cast blame, they are much more likely to blame borrowers:

The [national] increase in new foreclosures was mostly driven by problems in California, Florida, Nevada and Arizona, he [Doug Duncan, the Mortgage Bankers Association's chief economist] said. In those four states, foreclosures are being "heavily influenced by speculators who are walking away from properties . . . " Duncan said. 23 Articles on these opposing themes also differ in the experts they quote to discuss the problem. Markets theme articles quote economists and real estate professionals, who tend to downplay the problem, while intervention theme articles more often quote nonprofit representatives and consumer advocates, who tend to heighten the sense of crisis:

"The last year has been a pause, and that's OK," said veteran Realtor Steve Hyland, new president of the St. Paul Area Association of Realtors. 24 "Beth Kodluboy, executive director of the Minneapolis tenant advocacy group Home Line, has seen a steady increase in foreclosure-related calls . . . Cheryl Peterson, senior mortgage foreclosure prevention counselor for Twin Cities Habitat for Humanity, said she's been getting more and more calls from renters in the past year. "They don't know what to do," she said. 25 In addition to discussing the problem differently, articles on these opposing themes also discuss the policy solutions differently. Articles on the markets theme negatively portray most policy solutions, especially those that involve government intervention:

We are in an artificial recovery. It's government controlled and manipulated . . . Foreclosed homes, in particular, are being kept off the market through loan modification attempts and other policies . . . it's definitely standing in the way of the natural process. 26 "I can't see any quick fixes," said Bobby Ingels, a developer and new president of the Housing and Building Association of Colorado Springs. "I really think this mortgage thing has to work itself out." 27 Articles on the intervention theme, however, refer to interventions positively and often implore the government to intervene more:

What the state and particularly the federal agencies ought to be doing instead is finding ways for ordinary homeowners to get effective and timely mortgage modifications. 28 Overall, the markets topic reflects a long-standing theme on natural markets, including the view of unnatural government intervention. The intervention theme is an opposing theme that sympathizes with rather than blames borrowers and portrays policy solutions positively. Appendix B contains additional comparative excerpts from media data on these two discourse themes.

To include a measure of these topics in the event history analysis, I calculate a 75-day rolling average of the topic probabilities in states' media. On any given day, the topic prevalence measures reflect the mean topic probability from any articles published that day and the prior seventy-four days. This measure allows some time for media discourse to accumulate and work through either of the mechanisms discussed above (i.e., influencing legislators' own preferences or as an indication of voters' preferences) and for legislators to draft and begin to advance legislation through the legislative process. Seventy-five days is nevertheless arbitrary, and I run the full model with moving averages with windows of 30-, 45-, 60-, 100-, and 115-days. The results for the main predictors are the same and can be seen in Appendix C, Table C1. For the models predicting passing legislation (as opposed to introducing legislation), I test longer windows of 130-and 145-days. 29 A possible complication is that the discourse topics, as measured from state media, are simply picking up reporting on state foreclosure legislation, rather than reflecting and contributing to a larger discourse about the policy problem and policy solutions. I take several steps in designing the analysis to address this. One is having two policy outcome measures, including the earliest policy outcome, i.e., when legislation is introduced. State media is not as often aware of and reporting on state legislation that has not yet been introduced, because of the relatively little attention paid to state legislatures (Rosenthal 2004;Squire and Moncrief 2020). However, this may be different during a crisis (Rogers 2017), and state legislators themselves can use the media to bring attention to their legislative efforts, so I take additional steps.

I include a measure in the model of the total number of foreclosure articles for each state. If state media is more simply reporting on the legislation itself, or if what matters is greater discussion of the foreclosure crisis in general, then this measure will be positively associated with state legislators advancing legislation. Specific discourse themes would not matter above this and would not have opposing effects, as I predict with the intervention and markets topics.

Finally, I did a qualitative examination of about 200 articles to assess the validity of interpreting these topics, not just as intervention and markets themes, but as measures of a broader discourse, rather than as indicators of reporting on legislation. I find that episodic reporting on legislation is picked up with topic 13, which I labeled "legislation." There is an extended discussion on this qualitative analysis in Appendix B.

"They don't know what to do," she said. 25 In addition to discussing the problem differently, articles on these opposing themes also discuss the policy solutions differently. Articles on the markets theme negatively portray most policy solutions, especially those that involve government intervention:

We are in an artificial recovery. It's government controlled and manipulated . . . Foreclosed homes, in particular, are being kept off the market through loan modification attempts and other policies . . . it's definitely standing in the way of the natural process. 26 "I can't see any quick fixes," said Bobby Ingels, a developer and new president of the Housing and Building Association of Colorado Springs. "I really think this mortgage thing has to work itself out." 27 Articles on the intervention theme, however, refer to interventions positively and often implore the government to intervene more:

What the state and particularly the federal agencies ought to be doing instead is finding ways for ordinary homeowners to get effective and timely mortgage modifications. 28 Overall, the markets topic reflects a long-standing theme on natural markets, including the view of unnatural government intervention. The intervention theme is an opposing theme that sympathizes with rather than blames borrowers and portrays policy solutions positively. Appendix B contains additional comparative excerpts from media data on these two discourse themes.

To include a measure of these topics in the event history analysis, I calculate a 75-day rolling average of the topic probabilities in states' media. On any given day, the topic prevalence measures reflect the mean topic probability from any articles published that day and the prior seventy-four days. This measure allows some time for media discourse to accumulate and work through either of the mechanisms discussed above (i.e., influencing legislators' own preferences or as an indication of voters' preferences) and for legislators to draft and begin to advance legislation through the legislative process. Seventy-five days is nevertheless arbitrary, and I run the full model with moving averages with windows of 30-, 45-, 60-, 100-, and 115-days. The results for the main predictors are the same and can be seen in Appendix C, Table C1. For the models predicting passing legislation (as opposed to introducing legislation), I test longer windows of 130-and 145-days. 29 A possible complication is that the discourse topics, as measured from state media, are simply picking up reporting on state foreclosure legislation, rather than reflecting and contributing to a larger discourse about the policy problem and policy solutions. I take several steps in designing the analysis to address this. One is having two policy outcome measures, including the earliest policy outcome, i.e., when legislation is introduced. State media is not as often aware of and reporting on state legislation that has not yet been introduced, because of the relatively little attention paid to state legislatures (Rosenthal 2004;Squire and Moncrief 2020). However, this may be different during a crisis (Rogers 2017), and state legislators themselves can use the media to bring attention to their legislative efforts, so I take additional steps.

I include a measure in the model of the total number of foreclosure articles for each state. If state media is more simply reporting on the legislation itself, or if what matters is greater discussion of the foreclosure crisis in general, then this measure will be positively associated with state legislators advancing legislation. Specific discourse themes would not matter above this and would not have opposing effects, as I predict with the intervention and markets topics.

Finally, I did a qualitative examination of about 200 articles to assess the validity of interpreting these topics, not just as intervention and markets themes, but as measures of a broader discourse, rather than as indicators of reporting on legislation. I find that episodic reporting on legislation is picked up with topic 13, which I labeled "legislation." There is an extended discussion on this qualitative analysis in Appendix B.

To measure themes in states' foreclosure crisis discourse, I collected newspaper articles and news website text using Lexis-Nexis academic and ProQuest (for similar methodological approaches, see Boushey 2016). The media data include articles from more traditional and well-known newspapers such as the Los Angeles Times and the Chicago Tribune, as well as less well-known outlets including Louisiana's New Orleans City Business and Maine's Kennebec Journal. These data also include web-based outlets such as Washington state's The B-Town Blog. I collect all articles on foreclosure in all states, whether they advanced any foreclosure prevention or not. This is necessary to measure the discourse in states that do not advance legislation as well as those that do. I do not collect data from social media platforms, but I note that the articles constituting my data are often shared and circulated on social media. To identify articles on the foreclosure crisis, I searched for articles that had the words "foreclosure" or "foreclosures" in the headline, and those that were published between 1/1/2007 and 12/31/2011. This resulted in 20,962 articles. See Appendix A Table A1 for the full list of media outlets and the number of articles from each source.

To inductively code and quantify the themes in these text data, I use an unsupervised form of topic modeling called Structural Topic Modeling (Roberts et al. 2014), which builds on the traditional Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) by incorporating meta data to improve the identification of topics. 13 The meta data I include are the year and state in which the article was published. Unsupervised topic modeling allows researchers to take advantage of an inductive coding approach that is often used in qualitative analyses (DiMaggio 2015; Evans and Aceves 2016;Nelson 2020;Nelson et al. 2018).

In implementing an unsupervised topic model, the researcher decides on the number of topics that the model will estimate but does not influence the content of the topics. Quantitative methods exist for choosing the number of topics (e.g. Lee and Mimno 2014), though social scientists note that the solutions with substantively meaningful topics are frequently not the mathematically optimal solutions (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013; Nelson etal. 2018). Instead, the common approach is to estimate several solutions with varying numbers of topics and identify the solution with analytically useful topics. I followed this approach, and after examining the results with various numbers of topics (5-25), I chose the 15-topic solution. Further details on this choice are in Appendix A Section A2.

Identifying and labeling what the inductively discovered topics reflect requires the researcher's interpretation. I establish and validate my topic labels by considering three pieces of information: 1) the words constituting each topic with the highest probability; 2) the top FREX terms, which are a variation on the most probable words that consider the relative salience of terms in a given topic (Hausser and Strimmer 2009); and 3) my reading of articles that have a high probability of being constituted by particular topics, which allowed me to assess whether the articles' substantive content reflects my topic interpretation. See Table 1 below for lists of the eleven most probable words for each of the fifteen topics. Table 1 groups the topics into three categories: policy problem topics, episodic events topics, and what I call idiosyncratic foreclosure language topics. I discuss these categorizations more in the following paragraphs. To more fully demonstrate the meanings of these topics, Appendix A Table A2 shows a list of the top FREX terms, excerpts from articles that have a high probability of being constituted by each topic, as well as one complete article and the probabilities that it is constituted by each of the topics. 14 In an unsupervised model, there is no guarantee that topics of any number will map onto a researchers' analytical goals, because the topics will correspond to what structures the corpus (Chuang et al. 2013;Eads et al. 2021;McAuliffe and Blei 2008). The benefit of an unsupervised approach is allowing the inductive discovery of themes (Nelson 2020;Nelson et al. 2021). In a corpus of news articles, the primary structure is episodic reporting on events and many of the topics will likely reflect that (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013). I interpreted over half of the topics in the 15-topic solution as episodic event topics. I labeled these topics "families," "documentation," "fraud," "abandoned property," "counseling," "foreclosure activity," "legislation," and "minority families." I interpreted five other topics as reflecting the idiosyncratic language involved in discussing foreclosures. I labeled these "CRE" for commercial real estate, "court," "counties," "home sales," and "filler." This last reflects less substantively meaningful words, akin to "stop" words. See Table 1 for the most probable words in these topics and Appendix A Section A2 for an extended discussion of how I labeled and categorized each.

Additionally, this topic reflects discussions of policy solutions that are critical of state intervention and supportive of natural market-based solutions. This type of discourse could affect the public and policymakers' understanding of the severity of the problem or their sense of connection with it (Shiller 2017). More directly, it could negatively

paragraphs: As a measure of economic interests, I include a measure of the housing market conditions in each state. I use the Federal Housing Finance Agency's (FHFA) quarterly House Price Index. 30 This index considers single-family house prices based on repeat sales or refinancings. This is a normalized index, rather than absolute house prices, so the values are comparable across states. I measure the change in each state's HPI from the previous quarter and lag this measure by one quarter. See Figure 1 above for each state's change in housing price from their 2006 high.

paragraphs: To include political interests, I use measures for the political ideology of the state legislatures and the ideology of states' citizens. For state legislature ideology, I use Shor and McCarty's (2011) measure made available by Jordan and Grossmann's (2016) Correlates of State Policy Project. Shor and McCarty offer a yearly ideology score that ranges from -1 for liberal to 1 for conservative for the house and senate of each state. I take the mean ideology score between the two legislative chambers for a single legislature ideology measure per state-year. This single measure for both chambers allows me to include Nebraska in the analysis even though Nebraska's legislature is unicameral. For citizen ideology, I use Berry et al. (1998) measure, also made available via the State Correlates Project. This measure ranges from 0 to 100, with higher values indicating more a liberal citizenry. I adjusted this measure so that it matches the direction and scale of the state legislature ideology measure described above, with higher values indicating a more conservative citizenry.

paragraphs: States' ability to advance foreclosure prevention legislation could be affected by their fiscal capacity, as policies often included social spending to cover counselors, or incentives for lenders to refinance, or funds to assist borrowers with mortgage payments. State budgets often have specific constraints on spending and/or raising revenue (Squire and Moncrief 2020: Chpt. 6). I follow Soule and Zylan (1997) and include a measure of states' total yearly revenue minus total yearly expenditures. These data come from the U.S. Census Bureau Annual Survey of State Government Finances.

paragraphs: As a final measure, I include whether a state requires a judicial foreclosure process or allows non-judicial foreclosures, which is a major distinction in foreclosure processes among states. In judicial foreclosure states, the foreclosure process begins when lenders file suit against the borrower and the process then proceeds within the court system. This type of process takes longer than a non-judicial process in which lenders have fewer requirements before auctioning the property. This extra time is understood to benefit borrowers, who may be able to use the time to figure out a way to avoid foreclosure. Additionally, in a judicial foreclosure, there are lower barriers for borrowers to legally challenge the foreclosure. In a non-judicial foreclosure, on the other hand, the borrower has to be quick enough and have the legal wherewithal to either file for bankruptcy or begin their own suit in court before the lender auctions the property. I create an indicator for whether a state is a judicial or non-judicial foreclosure state following Alexander et al. (2011). There are nineteen judicial foreclosure states. 31 Table 2 shows the descriptive statistics for all measures included in the analysis.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: The goal of the analysis is to estimate the association between themes in the foreclosure discourse, political factors, and the likelihood of state legislatures advancing foreclosure prevention legislation across the crisis timeframe. As the main modeling strategy, I use a discrete-time logistic regression event history model (Allison 2014) to estimate the risk of introducing or passing legislation as a function of the prevalence of two discourse themes, political factors, and the elapsed duration of the crisis. Event history analysis is the best choice for modeling time-to-event data and is designed to appropriately handle rightcensoring (Allison 2014;Ezell, Land, and Cohen 2003;Powers 2010). Right censoring occurs when observation ends before all cases had the opportunity to experience an event and failing to account for this data structure can result in biased estimates (Allison 2014).

Because the dataset contains the exact date of each legislative action, I specify time at the day level, as days since January 1, 2007. For each state, the outcome variable equals 1 on the day that legislation advanced through either of the two milestones (being introduced or passing). The primary independent variables, foreclosure discourse, are also measured at the day level, corresponding to the day of media publication. Other variables are measured at larger intervals, quarterly or annually, and are forward filled.

I begin data collection at the beginning of 2007 because this is the year in which crisisrelated foreclosure legislation consideration began at the state level (Alexander et al. 2011); all states are immediately in the risk set on January 1, 2007 and are censored at the end of 2011. Since some states introduced or passed more than one piece of foreclosure prevention legislation, I allow states to contribute multiple events and adjust for this dependency with robust standard errors clustered at the state level (Allison 2014). I also include year fixed effects as year dummy variables. This allows controlling for the fact that the hazard rate of advancing legislation may vary with time (Allison 2014:12-13), as we might expect it would in a developing crisis. 32

Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: Figure 2 shows the prevalence of the market and intervention topics over time in each state.

The figure shows a 75-day moving average of the topic probabilities. Orange reflects the prevalence of the markets topic and purple reflects the intervention topic. The orange and purple numbers in the upper corners indicate the overall topic probability means over the whole crisis period. The figure also includes when states introduced (dotted vertical lines) or passed (solid vertical lines) foreclosure prevention legislation. There are several notable points. One is that both topics are reflected to some extent in most states. In many states, the overall mean topic prevalence is equal; in Georgia, the mean prevalence is .01 for both topics. In other states, one topic is more prevalent, on average. In California, the markets topic is more prevalent, with an overall topic probability mean of .07, compared to a mean of .05 for the intervention topic. The discourse in Arizona and Minnesota is similarly skewed toward the markets topic. However, in Minnesota, there is a clear shift from early in the crisis, when the intervention topic was more prevalent, to later when the markets topic began to dominate the discourse. In other states, the intervention topic is more prevalent on average, including Illinois, with an intervention topic mean of .03 and markets topic mean of .01. The question for the event history analysis in the next section is whether the prevalence of themes in the discourse is associated with state legislatures' likelihood of advancing foreclosure prevention legislation.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: Table 3 presents results of states' likelihood of introducing foreclosure prevention legislation and Table 4 presents results of states' likelihood of passing foreclosure prevention legislation. Table 3 presents three models of legislatures' likelihood of introducing foreclosure prevention legislation.

Model 1 in Table 3 is a baseline model that includes the economic measures, i.e., the change in the housing price index and states' fiscal spending capacity, and year fixed effects. The effect for the change in housing prices indicates that, with a positive change in housing prices, the likelihood of introducing legislation decreases, although the effect is not statistically significant. As states' fiscal capacity increases, the likelihood of introducing legislation also increases, but this effect is also not statistically significant. For years, compared to 2007, states were more likely to introduce legislation in all subsequent years, though only 2009 and 2011 have statistically significant effects in Model 1.

Model 2 in Table 3 adds in the political measures, the judicial foreclosure indicator, and the number of articles published on foreclosure. Most of the political measures have statistically significant effects. States whose citizens are maximally conservative are 54% (i.e., 1 À exp À 0:773 ð Þ) less likely than states whose citizens are maximally liberal to introduce foreclosure prevention legislation. When state legislatures are maximally conservative, they are 5% less likely to introduce foreclosure prevention legislation; however, this is not statistically significant. Both interest group measures have significant effects, in the opposing directions that we might expect, given the groups' interests. For each additional banking interest group that is active in a state, the legislature is 2% less likely to introduce legislation, while for each additional legal consumer interest group that is active, the legislature is 3% more likely to introduce legislation. Judicial foreclosure states are more than twice as likely to introduce foreclosure prevention legislation than non- judicial foreclosure states. Finally, for each additional article on foreclosure published in states' media, regardless of themes constituting the article, states are < 1% more likely to introduce legislation. Model 3 in Table 3 is the full model that adds in the rolling means of the prevalence of the markets and intervention topics in states' media. As expected, the topics have opposing effects. If the rolling average probability of the markets topic increases by .01 in a state's media -that is, if the prevalence increases from, say, 3% markets topic prevalence to 4%then a state's likelihood of introducing legislation decreases by 26%. If the rolling average prevalence of the intervention topic increases by .01 in a state's media, then a state's likelihood of introducing legislation increases by 87%.

Table 4 presents four models of legislatures' likelihood of passing foreclosure prevention legislation. 33 Model 1 includes the same 75-day rolling mean for the topic probabilities as was used to predict when legislation would be introduced. In this model, the discourse topics again have opposing effects, that is, the markets topic has a negative effect, and the intervention topic has a positive effect on the likelihood of passing legislation, though neither has a statistically significant effect.

Since passing legislation takes longer than introducing it, I tested expanded moving average windows. Model 2 shows the effects when using a moving average window of 130 days, Model 3 shows results with a window of 145 days, and Model 4 shows results with a window of 160 days. In all three models, the positive effect of the intervention topic is statistically significant, while the negative effect of the markets topic does not reach statistical significance across any of the expanded windows. I discuss a possible reason for this difference in statistical significance below. Overall, the results indicate that the prevalence of different discourse themes in states' media discourse can have contrasting associations with the likelihood of introducing and passing legislation while accounting for political factors.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: Despite growing calls to integrate policy discourse with political factors in examining policy outcomes (e.g., Padamsee 2009), few empirical studies have yet to do this. This study responds to this challenge by using computational text analysis techniques to measure discourse themes on a scale large enough to quantify the policy discourse across all fifty states and across five years of an important policymaking episode -the 2007-2011 foreclosure crisis in the United States. This approach addresses a critique of discourse and policy studies, namely, that while scholars argue that discourse affects policy outcomes, it is rarely tested against other factors (Campbell 2002;Yee 1996). The results of this study provide evidence that discourse themes can have an impact on policy outcomes, while taking into account other, well-established political influences.

This study also provides new evidence that the long-standing "neoliberal" discourse on economic problems continues to shape policy outcomes (Block 1996;Campbell and Pedersen 2001;Lee Mudge 2008;Strang and Bradburn 2001). By examining two milestones in the policymaking process, the findings here contribute further to this literature in suggesting that the opposing sides of this discourse may be most influential at different stages of the policymaking process. Purveyors of the markets theme may be most effective at keeping policies from being introduced in the first place. However, once policies are introduced, the markets theme may not be as effective in preventing the policy from passing. Purveyors of the intervention theme, on the other hand, may be more likely to have a positive impact on moving policy forward at both stages. This conclusion is tentative and requires additional research. As I discuss at length in Appendix C, the articles that include the intervention discourse and discussions of policy solutions more obviously frame the policy solutions positively. This contrasts with the articles that include the markets discourse and discussions of policy solutions, which, while discussing the policy problem in market discourse terms, tended to be more ambiguous on their support for or opposition to the policy solutions. If both topics reflect discussions of the policy problem according to their opposing discourses, but only the intervention discourse is used to frame legislation, that may account for the markets topic losing its impact in the later stages of policymaking. But the question remains, if market discourse proponents had used the discourse to frame potential policy solutions more negatively, would they have been more successful at blocking legislation from passing? Perhaps these proponents worried that use of the markets rhetoric against specific policies would have backfired? These are questions for future research.

The findings here corroborate and deepen emerging results in political theory. Prior research found that, during economic crises, support for state intervention increased across the ideological spectrum among the public (Page Benjamin and Shapiro Robert 1992;Schlozman et al. 2005). This study, however, finds that states with conservative publics were less likely to advance foreclosure prevention legislation. This corroborates Brooks and Manza's (2013) findings that public support for policy intervention during this crisis was ideologically rather than economically driven. Taken together, these findings suggest a growing political polarization among the public that is impacting policymaking at multiple levels of government.

There are some limitations in this study that could be addressed in future research. A key limitation is the reliance on media publications as the measure of discourse. While using media in such measures is a common operationalization and has a theoretical basis as a "master forum" (Boushey 2016;Ferree 2002;Gamson 2004), it is still not ideal in this case. The ideal textual data for this analysis would be legislators' discourse on the foreclosure crisis, say from legislature committee meetings. However, the statistical analysis requires data that include all legislative discussions across the analytical period for all fifty states, including discussions that did not result in the advancement of legislation. Most states neither collect nor make these data available. If such data, or a satisfactory proxy for them, were to become available, this would allow future researchers to test for the two mechanisms of discourse influence -by shaping policymakers' own understanding of the policy problem or policymakers' responding to the perception of the public's understandings and policy preferences. Further, such data would allow researchers to examine the advancement of legislation through committees, which is another critical policy advancement milestone (Squire and Moncrief 2020). Committees often hear directly from the public and special interest groups about the policy problem (Squire and Moncrief 2020). However, media coverage may still impact the discourse at this stage. During the foreclosure crisis, one Texas committee chair commented during a committee meeting on how prevalent the "subprime crisis" issue was in the media. Further, this committee chair reflected the markets discourse theme in saying that, "we're trying to figure out if there is anything we should do. Sometimes it's better not to do anything." 34 Examining the independent effect of discourse and political factors at the committee stage is an important next step for future research.

Additionally, there is an important scope condition for the current study, which examines policymaking during a crisis. Policy discourse may be particularly influential during such a "spectacular focusing event" because crises often disrupt the status quo and create opportunities (Béland 2005:7). Yet, crisis scholars also note that sometimes the status quo holds, sometimes policymakers are lauded for their efforts and other times they suffer a backlash (Boin, 't Hart, and Allan 2009;Brooks and Manza 2013). This uncertainty may make policymakers particularly sensitive to the influence of discourse. Future studies could use the methods of this paper to examine discourse and political factors in settled times to provide evidence for whether discourse has similar independent influences as during a crisis. With an accumulation of such research, we could begin to untangle what conditions of policymaking episodes indicate discourse or political factors or both will impact policy outcomes.

This study also provides new evidence that the long-standing "neoliberal" discourse on economic problems continues to shape policy outcomes (Block 1996;Campbell and Pedersen 2001;Lee Mudge 2008;Strang and Bradburn 2001). By examining two milestones in the policymaking process, the findings here contribute further to this literature in suggesting that the opposing sides of this discourse may be most influential at different stages of the policymaking process. Purveyors of the markets theme may be most effective at keeping policies from being introduced in the first place. However, once policies are introduced, the markets theme may not be as effective in preventing the policy from passing. Purveyors of the intervention theme, on the other hand, may be more likely to have a positive impact on moving policy forward at both stages. This conclusion is tentative and requires additional research. As I discuss at length in Appendix C, the articles that include the intervention discourse and discussions of policy solutions more obviously frame the policy solutions positively. This contrasts with the articles that include the markets discourse and discussions of policy solutions, which, while discussing the policy problem in market discourse terms, tended to be more ambiguous on their support for or opposition to the policy solutions. If both topics reflect discussions of the policy problem according to their opposing discourses, but only the intervention discourse is used to frame legislation, that may account for the markets topic losing its impact in the later stages of policymaking. But the question remains, if market discourse proponents had used the discourse to frame potential policy solutions more negatively, would they have been more successful at blocking legislation from passing? Perhaps these proponents worried that use of the markets rhetoric against specific policies would have backfired? These are questions for future research.

The findings here corroborate and deepen emerging results in political theory. Prior research found that, during economic crises, support for state intervention increased across the ideological spectrum among the public (Page Benjamin and Shapiro Robert 1992;Schlozman et al. 2005). This study, however, finds that states with conservative publics were less likely to advance foreclosure prevention legislation. This corroborates Brooks and Manza's (2013) findings that public support for policy intervention during this crisis was ideologically rather than economically driven. Taken together, these findings suggest a growing political polarization among the public that is impacting policymaking at multiple levels of government.

There are some limitations in this study that could be addressed in future research. A key limitation is the reliance on media publications as the measure of discourse. While using media in such measures is a common operationalization and has a theoretical basis as a "master forum" (Boushey 2016;Ferree 2002;Gamson 2004), it is still not ideal in this case. The ideal textual data for this analysis would be legislators' discourse on the foreclosure crisis, say from legislature committee meetings. However, the statistical analysis requires data that include all legislative discussions across the analytical period for all fifty states, including discussions that did not result in the advancement of legislation. Most states neither collect nor make these data available. If such data, or a satisfactory proxy for them, were to become available, this would allow future researchers to test for the two mechanisms of discourse influence -by shaping policymakers' own understanding of the policy problem or policymakers' responding to the perception of the public's understandings and policy preferences. Further, such data would allow researchers to examine the advancement of legislation through committees, which is another critical policy advancement milestone (Squire and Moncrief 2020). Committees often hear directly from the public and special interest groups about the policy problem (Squire and Moncrief 2020). However, media coverage may still impact the discourse at this stage. During the foreclosure crisis, one Texas committee chair commented during a committee meeting on how prevalent the "subprime crisis" issue was in the media. Further, this committee chair reflected the markets discourse theme in saying that, "we're trying to figure out if there is anything we should do. Sometimes it's better not to do anything." 34 Examining the independent effect of discourse and political factors at the committee stage is an important next step for future research.

Additionally, there is an important scope condition for the current study, which examines policymaking during a crisis. Policy discourse may be particularly influential during such a "spectacular focusing event" because crises often disrupt the status quo and create opportunities (Béland 2005:7). Yet, crisis scholars also note that sometimes the status quo holds, sometimes policymakers are lauded for their efforts and other times they suffer a backlash (Boin, 't Hart, and Allan 2009;Brooks and Manza 2013). This uncertainty may make policymakers particularly sensitive to the influence of discourse. Future studies could use the methods of this paper to examine discourse and political factors in settled times to provide evidence for whether discourse has similar independent influences as during a crisis. With an accumulation of such research, we could begin to untangle what conditions of policymaking episodes indicate discourse or political factors or both will impact policy outcomes.
sections.preview_head: Despite growing calls to integrate policy discourse with political factors in examining policy outcomes (e.g., Padamsee 2009), few empirical studies have yet to do this. This study responds to this challenge by using computational text analysis techniques to measure discourse themes on a scale large enough to quantify the policy discourse across all fifty states and across five years of an important policymaking episode -the 2007-2011 foreclosure crisis in the United States. This approach addresses a critique of discourse and policy studies, namely, that while scholars argue that discourse affects policy outcomes, it is rarely tested against other factors (Campbell 2002;Yee 1996).

Additionally, scholars have previously asked why the federal government did not do more to prevent the crisis in the first place and why they did not see it coming (Fligstein, Brundage, and Schultz 2017;Gieve and Provost 2012). 5. For the National Consumer Law Center (NCLC), "State Foreclosure Laws & Policy" is one of their "key issues." 6. The two bills were: 1) the American Housing Rescue and Foreclosure Prevention Act, due to implementation obstacles, only 340 mortgages were covered under the program (Immergluck 2013); and 2) the Emergency Economic Stabilization Act, also known as the "bank bailout" bill. 7. Brooks and Manza (2013) use GSS survey data, which asks respondents about abstract federal government interventions, not specific policies. Political ideology was driving the public's policy preferences at least with respect to abstract interventions from "the government in Washington." 8. Another influential stream in the discourse literature examines what shapes the discourse but is less concerned with policy outcomes (Bail 2012;Benford and Snow 2000;Campbell 2002;Ferree 2002;Fiss and Hirsch 2005;Gamson and Modigliani 1989;Schneider and Sidney 2009). 9. The free-market theme was apparent in elite discourse among academics during the crisis (e.g. Stiglitz 2010) and Lee Mudge (2008) notes that, in the past, this discourse has permeated among a "broader array of actors" (pg. 705). One study identifies a "self-regulating markets" theme in the New York Times during the crisis (Schranz and Eisenegger 2011). To clarify the different contributions of Schranz and Eisenegger (2011) and the current paper, they do not measure policy outcomes; their focus is on the crisis discourse itself, as measured in select media. 10. Research Report No. 365, "Housing Foreclosures in Kentucky," by Spurlock et al. July 9, 2009. 11. U.S. states passed 325 pieces of legislation related to foreclosures between 2007 and 2011. Some were major new laws but had no bearing on current foreclosures; for example, instituting licensing for brokers, which would only affect future mortgages. Others were minor adjustments to existing laws; for example, Indiana removed the requirement that borrower notices be on "canary yellow" paper.

Kate Watkins, the Chief Economist for the Colorado state legislature's nonpartisan research agency. The author also thanks the participants in the political culture session at the 2018 ASA Annual Conference for feedback on earlier drafts of the manuscript.

sections.paper_id: Eads - 2025 - Some States Stepping In Politics and Discourse in Foreclosure Prevention Legislation Outcomes Durin
paragraphs: Alicia Eads is an Assistant Professor at the University of Toronto, cross-appointed in Sociology and the Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources. Her research includes how cultural factors, including discourse and moral judgements, shaped the policy responses to the foreclosure and global financial crisis in the U.S. among different types of government organizations.
